payload.title: Better Buses Action Plan
payload.description: NYC DOT's 2019 plan to improve bus speeds citywide by 25% through bus lanes, TSP, enforcement, and bus stop improvements

payload.entity_name: New York City Department of Transportation
payload.name: NYC DOT
MTA
payload.description: MTA operates NYC Transit and MTA Bus Company bus services in New York City

payload.name: MTA Bus Company
NYPD

payload.claim_text: improving bus speeds for all New Yorkers by 25 percent, rising from their current average speed of eight miles an hour
payload.statement: Goal to increase average bus speeds by 25% by end of 2020
payload.description: Mayor de Blasio sets bold goal to improve bus speeds by 25% from 8 mph baseline

payload.claim_text: Increase average speeds by 25% by the end of 2020
payload.statement: Action Plan goal: increase average speeds by 25% by end of 2020

payload.raw_value_text: nearly 28,000 fewer hours on the bus each day
payload.description: Estimated daily time savings citywide if 25% speed improvement achieved

payload.raw_value_text: 8.0 m.p.h. was the average speed calculated for all buses (weekday peak and off-peak) both over all 12 months of 2018 and in the latest 'typical' traffic month - October
payload.description: Baseline average bus speed citywide for 2018

payload.raw_value_text: target 10.0 m.p.h. by the end of 2020
payload.description: Target average bus speed by end of 2020

payload.raw_value_text: ridership has decreased by 13% between 2014 and 2018
payload.description: Citywide bus ridership decline 2014-2018

payload.claim_text: New York City buses are, in fact, the slowest in the United States, averaging 8 miles per hour
payload.statement: NYC buses are the slowest in the US at 8 mph average

payload.raw_value_text: puts 94% of City residents within walking distance of a bus stop
payload.description: Percentage of NYC residents within walking distance of a bus stop

payload.raw_value_text: New Yorkers ride the bus over two million times each weekday
payload.description: Daily bus ridership citywide

payload.raw_value_text: implement DOT street design projects that benefit 600,000 daily riders
payload.description: Daily riders benefiting from 2019 DOT street design projects

payload.raw_value_text: ON AVERAGE, SBS ROUTES ARE 27% FASTER THAN LOCAL/LIMITED ROUTES
payload.description: SBS routes are 27% faster than local/limited routes on average

payload.raw_value_text: 18 SBS routes have opened on 16 corridors
payload.description: SBS routes opened since 2008

payload.raw_value_text: the Department of Transportation has built 111 miles of bus lanes
payload.description: Cumulative bus lane mileage built by DOT as of April 2019

payload.raw_value_text: New York has 2.5 times more bus riders than any other U.S. City
payload.description: NYC bus ridership compared to other US cities

payload.raw_value_text: Add 300 Transit Signal Priority (TSP) intersections per year
payload.description: Annual TSP intersection installation target

payload.raw_value_text: Currently there are nearly 600 intersections with TSP
payload.description: Existing TSP intersections as of April 2019

payload.raw_value_text: Install 10–15 miles of new bus lanes per year
payload.description: Annual new bus lane installation target

payload.raw_value_text: Improve 5 miles of existing bus lanes per year
payload.description: Annual existing bus lane improvement target

payload.raw_value_text: Pilot up to 2 miles of physically separated bus lanes in 2019
payload.description: 2019 pilot target for physically separated bus lanes

payload.raw_value_text: Increased NYPD bus lane enforcement with seven dedicated tow truck teams
payload.description: NYPD dedicated tow truck teams for bus lane enforcement

payload.claim_text: Most SBS routes include the following components: Red bus lanes, Transit Signal Priority, Stop spacing longer than limited routes, Off-board fare collection, Enhanced bus stops with large shelters, fare payment machines, and Real Time Passenger Information, High-capacity, low-floor buses, Unique branding

payload.raw_value_text: DOT will improve accessibility at 10 bus stops in 2019
payload.description: Bus stop accessibility improvements planned for 2019

payload.raw_value_text: nearly 500 bus stops currently have real time bus arrival information, with plans for nearly 200 more
payload.description: Existing real time passenger information signs at bus stops

payload.raw_value_text: MTA plans to buy more than 1,300 buses by 2022, including 60 all-electric buses
payload.description: MTA bus purchase plan by 2022 with 60 electric buses

payload.claim_text: The MTA uses Bus Time GPS tracking technology, which measures the location and speed of all buses in passenger service. To calculate average speed, the MTA measures the amount of time required for each bus run to travel from its first stop to its last, divided by the mileage of the route's path between those stops.
payload.statement: Methodology for calculating average bus speeds using MTA Bus Time GPS data

payload.description: Add pavement markings, extend northbound curbside bus lane to Northern Blvd, adjust signal timings, install signage for Bus and Truck Only corridor

payload.description: Install two new traffic signals, signalized crossings with pedestrian signals and high-visibility crosswalks, painted medians and curb extensions

payload.name: Livingston St, Boerum Pl to Flatbush Ave
payload.description: Add dedicated westbound right turn arrow, refresh existing bus lanes and extend hours, upgrade to protected bus lanes with physical barriers

payload.name: Malcolm X Blvd / Utica Ave, Chauncey St to Atlantic Ave
payload.description: Install curbside southbound bus lane and queue jump signal phases for northbound and southbound B46 buses at Utica Ave and Atlantic Ave

payload.name: Church Ave, E 7th St to Flatbush Ave
payload.description: Install curbside bus lanes, update curb regulations for truck loading and metered parking, investigate turn bans/bays, adjust bus stops

payload.name: East New York Ave, Herkimer St to Fulton St
payload.description: Clarify travel lanes and turning movements with new markings, modify signal timings, investigate turn restrictions, remove parking near intersections

payload.name: Narrows Rd at Hylan Blvd
payload.description: Relocate bus stops, reverse direction of Hylan Blvd east leg, investigate leading pedestrian signal, coordinate with NYSDOT for bus stop relocation

payload.claim_text: Bx6 South Bronx Crosstown corridor: 24,000 daily riders, eight subway lines, Metro-North, twenty bus routes. 76% of households within quarter-mile no vehicle. 56 minutes for 4.8 miles. Improvements on 161st St: two-way center-running bus lanes, boarding islands, sidewalk extensions, bus-only tunnel. Speed improvements: 39%-45% eastbound, 15%-26% westbound.
payload.statement: Bx6 on 161st Street precedent case study showing major speed improvements from bus priority treatments

payload.claim_text: Congestion pricing below 60th Street starting in 2021. The City's Better Buses Action Plan includes investments that complement congestion pricing by making transit trips within and into the Manhattan congestion pricing zone faster and more reliable.
payload.statement: Congestion pricing below 60th Street starting in 2021, complemented by Better Buses investments

payload.claim_text: MTA's Fast Forward Plan includes Bus Network Redesigns for all five boroughs. MTA will remove too-closely spaced bus stops starting in 2019 in the Bronx. MTA will redesign bus routes in all boroughs by 2022. NYC DOT's bus priority efforts will be closely coordinated.
payload.statement: Coordination with MTA Bus Network Redesign - stop consolidation starting 2019 in Bronx, full redesign by 2022

payload.claim_text: MTA is currently improving bus performance through service management including: mid-route driver changeover evaluation, 200 schedule reviews in 2019, service management on 17 corridors in 2019, new bus command center fully operational in 2021
payload.statement: MTA service management improvements under Fast Forward Plan

payload.claim_text: MTA plans to replace the MetroCard with OMNY. In 2019 MTA will introduce tap readers and install them on all buses by the end of 2020. MTA plans to implement all-door boarding as part of its OMNY strategy.
payload.statement: OMNY fare payment transition and all-door boarding plans

payload.description: Bus Lanes separate buses from general traffic, improving speed and reliability. Typically located along the curb or offset from the curb.
Extending hours, converting unpainted to red painted lanes, changing curbside lanes to offset lanes, and other measures that improve efficacy of bus lanes
Protecting a bus lane with a barrier makes it more difficult for vehicles other than buses to illegally use the lane. Must be accompanied by measures that preserve essential access to the curb.

Bus bulbs (bus boarders) are permanent sidewalk extensions at bus stops that provide more space for waiting passengers and allow buses to pull up to the curb without leaving the travel lane. DOT has recently been employing durable recycled plastic bus boarders.
Dedicated bus signal phases that provide buses with a dedicated signal phase allowing them to bypass congested general traffic and get a head start at intersections
TSP helps buses move through intersections faster by holding a traffic light green, or turning the light green sooner
Improving general traffic flow can benefit buses, including signal timing adjustments and improved lane markings
Evaluation of curb regulations including truck loading, passenger drop-off, and parking regulations to reduce double parking and illegal bus lane use
Under Vision Zero, bus priority projects provide opportunities for bus bulbs, new crosswalks, and median refuge islands to improve safety for all pedestrians
Ensuring sidewalks and pedestrian ramps are present and in good condition at bus stops
Limiting certain turns to increase safety and allow buses to avoid conflicts and move faster

payload.claim_text: NYC DOT will pilot two miles of physical barriers to further discourage illegal bus lane use at critical locations in 2019. Potential barrier treatments: concrete 'Jersey' barriers, rubber curb, plastic bollards, and concrete median curbs. Periodic gaps for emergency vehicle access and bus overtaking.
payload.statement: Two-mile protected bus lane pilot in 2019 with various barrier treatments

payload.claim_text: DOT will form a Better Buses Advisory Group to both inform program planning and support implementation. The Advisory Group will include City agencies, MTA, elected officials, transit advocates, and others.
payload.statement: Better Buses Advisory Group formation announced

payload.raw_value_text: converted to Bus and Truck Only southbound in March 2017, resulting in a 23% improvement in bus speeds
payload.description: Main St Flushing bus speed improvement after Bus and Truck Only conversion

payload.claim_text: The City currently has legal authority to install bus lane cameras along 16 corridors. We will advocate for authority to expand this enforcement approach to other routes.
payload.statement: Bus lane camera enforcement authority on 16 corridors

payload.claim_text: MTA tested bus-mounted cameras to identify and ticket bus lane violators and will roll out a larger pilot in summer 2019.
payload.statement: MTA on-bus camera enforcement pilot in summer 2019

payload.claim_text: Project outreach methods include: Stakeholder Briefings, On-Street Outreach, Public Workshops and Open Houses, Business Survey, Shoppers Survey

payload.description: Lexington Ave project from 96th St to 60th St
